segments.title: Taiwan invasion trade and geography constraints
segments.summary: The opening frames a Taiwan invasion scenario around deployable force, global trade disruption, nearby Japanese islands, and geography that complicates both attack and defense.
segments.body: The discussion captures the lecture's organizing argument rather than every later scenario branch. Dr. Clarke starts from a patron prompt about a possible Chinese invasion of Taiwan, then stresses that raw force totals matter less than deployable force, that Taiwan sits amid major shipping routes, and that nearby Japanese islands, air defenses, sensors, and anti-ship systems make the geography more complicated than a simple distance-to-China map suggests.

evidence.note: The introduction frames the patron question and says deployable force matters more than raw totals when assessing an invasion of Taiwan.
The opening analysis links Taiwan's busy trade routes, global economic effects, nearby Japanese-held islands, air defenses, sensors, and anti-ship weapons to the difficulty of the scenario.
